title: Cirrus: Collect benchmarks on machine instances
body:
The hardware used for podman-machine testing is fairly stable/predictable because it's bare-metal.  This is a nearly ideal environment for collection of benchmarking data.  Arrange for that to happen, and the resulting data to be collected.

Signed-off-by: Chris Evich <cevich@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body: @edsantiago @vrothberg we've got some [data being generated now (example)](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/4534778473480192/benchmark/data/benchmarks.txt).  However it doesn't seem very useful in that format.  Does/can that `localbenchmarks` target output to a file with the data so we don't need to scrape stdout?  Thoughts?
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/6c37cf332439989148e408af49afaf7c0cb6e166/hack/parse-localbenchmarks

That's an option we could use.  I'm having "how hard could it be" thoughts of outputting right from golang into a file/files.  OTOH, I don't want to disturb the measurements by doing that.

Also..."how hard could it be" often guarantees weeks of work and bickering over code quality.  Maybe better to just use @edsantiago perl that only he can read anyway :rofl:

I guess my main motivation for wanting to craft the output from the source is my golang-interest (a.k.a. for the funzies).  It would also be easier for a wider-community of developers to access/change/modify/fix/etc.

Unless there are strong opinions one way or another, I'll probably ponder this for a few days, then throw up my hands and just use the perl.

@edsantiago help please (when you get a chance).  The [raw benchmarks output artifact](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6036788599324672) (task link) looks right, but the parsed one is definitely wrong.  [Am I running it wrong](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16449/files#diff-87b53ab90b08478b1624b8f218f6d0b6eab834f2cf81dd73581540c3a0dfb63cR324-R325)?

@vrothberg & @edsantiago PTAL this is ready to go.  You'll find the benchmarks.csv and environment file are both now included in [the artifacts archive.](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5442393579716608).  This should make them easy to retrieve by secondary tooling per-task, and for the latest run on any branch via the artifacts Cirrus-API.

CI failure is our nemesis the shellchecker:
```
�[1mIn ./contrib/cirrus/lib.sh line 311:�[0m
�[0mINST_TYPE=$EC2_INST_TYPE  # one day may include other cloud's VM types.�[0m
�[33m          ^------------^ SC2154: EC2_INST_TYPE is referenced but not assigned.�[0m
```
Out of curiosity, why do we use `--color=always`?

> Out of curiosity, why do we use --color=always?

oh, it's a copy-paste from what I have in a `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, I can kill it.
okay...once again for the fourteenth time...please behave shellcheck...
Don't re-push yet, though: let's see if CI passes. I don't see a fix for the CI-break from a few pushes ago, so it might still need more fixes.

Aha, thanks. This is the error:
```
/bin/sh: line 1: ./test/tools/build/ginkgo: No such file or directory
```
This seems like one way to fix it:
```diff
diff --git a/Makefile b/Makefile
index 71c0d045f..9241d3071 100644
--- a/Makefile
+++ b/Makefile
@@ -581,7 +581,7 @@ localmachine: test-binaries
 	$(MAKE) ginkgo-run GINKGONODES=1 GINKGOWHAT=pkg/machine/e2e/. HACK=
 
 .PHONY: localbenchmarks
-localbenchmarks: test-binaries
+localbenchmarks: test-binaries .install.ginkgo
 	PATH=$(PATH):$(shell pwd)/hack ACK_GINKGO_RC=true $(GINKGO) \
 		      -focus "Podman Benchmark Suite" \
 		      -tags "$(BUILDTAGS) benchmarks" -noColor \
```

> /bin/sh: line 1: ./test/tools/build/ginkgo: No such file or directory

Ugh, no won't work, the binary moved.  I thought I fixed that problem in another PR...

> no won't work

Actually that might work.  Let me try cherry-picking the more permanent fix first, if it fails we'll try adding the ginkgo install to the benchmark target.  The trouble with this fix is we end up with two ginkgo's installed and at least one already in $PATH (which will drive developers crazy if they differ in behavior).
Oh bother!  I am not having a smooth week :disappointed:

Still failing the same way:
```
/bin/sh: line 1: ./test/tools/build/ginkgo: No such file or directory
```
I'm stumped. Only thing I can think of that will help is `set -x`.

Artifacts uploaded, though, and oops, the `sort` was a bad idea. Sorry. It needs to be removed.
argh, what a hell of a PR.  I may save this one for next year, a fresh mind, and `get_ci_vm.sh` :grinning:
FFS it's finally working.  And collecting properly formatted CSV data (thanks @edsantiago !).
status: This is waiting for final review/approval of Ed (who's on PTO).
LGTM but this is too old and scary to merge now. I'll slash-lgtm it in the morning, when I'll be around to monitor for problems.

gulp

/lgtm
